summary: This notebook implements a high-performance C++ optimizer for a geometric packing problem, specifically arranging 15-vertex Christmas tree shapes into a square for varying group sizes (N=1 to 200). It combines a log-barrier gradient descent with stochastic random walks and complex orbital moves to minimize the bounding box side length squared divided by N. The Python components handle compilation, execution, high-precision validation using Shapely, and automatic repair of any invalid configurations by falling back to a provided baseline blend. Readers should understand how to combine custom C++ geometry engines with Python validation pipelines for competitive programming optimization tasks.
competition_flow: Loads an initial tree packing configuration from a CSV, optimizes positions and rotations for each group size N using a custom C++ hybrid gradient/stochastic solver, validates the result for overlaps using high-precision Python geometry, and automatically reverts any invalid groups to the original baseline.
data_reading: Reads initial configuration from /kaggle/input/team-optimization-blend/submission.csv using C++ ifstream and string parsing, extracting id, x, y, and deg fields.
Python validation reads the output CSV using pandas.read_csv.
data_processing: C++ code applies rotation matrices and translations to a fixed 15-vertex polygon template.
Python code parses 's' prefixed strings to floats, calculates bounding boxes, and computes scores.
High-precision validation uses decimal.Decimal with 25-digit precision and shapely for geometric operations.
insights: A hybrid optimization approach combining log-barrier gradient descent with stochastic random walks effectively minimizes bounding box sizes for geometric packing problems.
High-precision arithmetic and spatial indexing are critical for reliable overlap detection in competitive geometry tasks where standard floats cause precision errors.
Implementing an automatic fallback to a baseline configuration guarantees submission validity when the optimizer encounters edge cases.
